answers.response:
To upgrade a settlement to a **city** in Catan, you need to follow these specific requirements:

### 1. Resources Required
You must spend a total of **5 resource cards**:
* **3 Ore** (Stone)
* **2 Wheat** (Grain)

### 2. Building Rules
* **Upgrade Only:** You cannot build a city from scratch. You must already have a **Settlement** on the board to "upgrade."
* **Location:** When you upgrade, you remove your settlement piece and replace it with the city piece on the same intersection.
* **Piece Limit:** You are limited by the number of city pieces in your color (usually 4). If you have all 4 cities on the board, you cannot build another until one is destroyed (which is rare in the base game).

---

### Benefits of a City
Upgrading is a major strategic move because it doubles your efficiency and increases your score:

* **Double Production:** When the number for an adjacent hex is rolled, you collect **2 resource cards** instead of 1.
* **Victory Points:** Each city is worth **2 Victory Points** (Settlements are only worth 1). 

### Strategic Tip
Since cities require a lot of Ore and Wheat, players often refer to these as "City Resources." If you plan on building cities early, try to place your initial settlements on high-probability Ore (e.g., 6 or 8) and Wheat hexes.

In **Catan**, if you run out of wooden (or plastic) road pieces in your supply, you simply **cannot build any more roads.**

The game's components are intended to be physical limits on what you can achieve. Here is a breakdown of how this affects your gameplay:

* **Hard Limit:** You start with 15 roads. Once all 15 are on the board, you cannot build a 16th road, even if you have the necessary resources (brick and lumber).
* **No "Upgrading" or Moving:** Unlike some other games, you cannot remove an existing road from the board to place it elsewhere. Once a road is placed, it is permanent for the duration of the game.
* **Longest Road:** If you have used all 15 roads and another player manages to build a continuous path of 16 or more (assuming they haven't reached their limit yet), they can take the **Longest Road** trophy from you. You would be unable to reclaim it because you cannot extend your path further.
* **Expansion Bottleneck:** Running out of roads effectively caps your ability to reach new settlement spots. If you haven't secured enough victory points by the time you hit your limit, you'll have to rely on upgrading to cities, buying Development Cards, or securing the Largest Army to win.

**Pro-Tip:** If you see you’re down to your last two or three roads, start prioritizing settlement placement or city upgrades rather than "scouting" across the board!

Yes, you can play multiple **Victory Point (VP)** development cards on the same turn. In fact, Victory Point cards are the only development cards that do not follow the standard "one card per turn" rule.

Here is how they work according to the official rules:

* **The Reveal:** You do not technically "play" VP cards during the game like you would a Knight or a Road Building card. Instead, you keep them hidden until you reach **10 points** (including the points from those cards).
* **Winning the Game:** Once you have enough points to win on your turn, you reveal all your VP cards at once to claim victory. 
* **The Exception:** Unlike other development cards, you can reveal VP cards even if you bought them on that same turn, provided they bring your total score to 10 or more.

### Summary of Differences
| Card Type | Limit Per Turn | Must be held for 1 turn? | Visible to others? |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Action Cards** (Knight, Monopoly, etc.) | Only 1 | Yes | When played |
| **Victory Point Cards** | No limit | No (only if winning) | Only when you win |

In **Catan** (formerly *The Settlers of Catan*), the Robber is a central mechanic used to disrupt opponents and limit resource production. It can be activated in two primary ways:

### 1. Rolling a Seven
The most common way the Robber moves is when a player rolls a **7** on the two six-sided dice during the resource production phase.
* **No Resource Production:** No hexes produce resources when a 7 is rolled.
* **The Hand Limit:** Any player with **8 or more resource cards** in their hand must choose half of them (rounded down) and discard them to the bank.
* **Relocation:** The player who rolled the 7 must move the Robber to any other terrain hex (or the desert).
* **Stealing:** The player then steals one random resource card from any opponent who has a settlement or city adjacent to the Robber's new hex.

### 2. Playing a Knight Card
A player can activate the Robber by playing a **Knight Development Card** during their turn.
* **Timing:** A Knight can be played at any time during the player's turn, even *before* rolling the dice.
* **Relocation & Stealing:** Just like rolling a 7, the player moves the Robber to a new hex and steals one random resource from an adjacent opponent.
* **Army Size:** The played Knight remains face-up on the table and counts toward the "Largest Army" bonus (worth 2 Victory Points).

---

### Key Robber Rules to Remember
* **Blocking Production:** The hex where the Robber is currently standing **cannot produce resources**. If that number is rolled, players with adjacent settlements receive nothing until the Robber is moved.
* **The Desert:** The Robber starts the game in the Desert hex. While it can be moved back there to "neutralize" it, doing so means you don't get to steal a card from anyone.
* **Mandatory Movement:** You **must** move the Robber to a different hex than the one it currently occupies; you cannot leave it in place to "reactivate" it.

In **Catan**, the game ends immediately when a player reaches **10 Victory Points** on their own turn.

While the rule is straightforward, there are a few specific conditions to keep in mind regarding how the game concludes:

* **Active Turn Requirement:** You can only win during your own turn. If you reach 10 points due to an action taken by another player (for example, if another player loses the "Longest Road" and it passes to you), you must wait until the start of your next turn to be declared the winner.
* **Instant Victory:** As soon as a player hits the 10-point threshold, the game stops. There is no "final round" for other players to try and catch up.
* **Point Sources:** Victory points are calculated by adding up:
    * **Settlements:** 1 point each.
    * **Cities:** 2 points each.
    * **Longest Road:** 2 points (requires at least 5 continuous road segments).
    * **Largest Army:** 2 points (requires at least 3 Knight cards played).
    * **Victory Point Cards:** 1 point each (kept hidden until the player can declare victory).

---

> **Note:** If you are playing an expansion like *Cities & Knights* or *Seafarers*, the point requirement often increases (typically to **13 points** or more) to account for the additional ways to score.

No, you cannot execute this specific trade scheme in **Catan**.

While the rules allow for a lot of wheeling and dealing, the official Catan Rulebook and subsequent clarifications from the designers (the "Compendium") prohibit this for two main reasons:

### 1. The "No Giveaways" Rule
Catan explicitly forbids **giving away cards**. A trade must involve a legitimate exchange of different resource types between players. The scheme you described results in Player A simply giving Player B one sheep for nothing in return. 

### 2. The "No Identical Trade" Rule
The rules state that you cannot trade the **same type of resource** back and forth in a single transaction. Trading 1 wheat for 1 wheat (or a sequence that results in the same outcome) is considered an "invalid trade."

---

### Why is this restricted?
The game is designed to prevent "kingmaking" or collusion. If players could freely give resources away, two players could easily pool all their assets to ensure one of them wins, which breaks the competitive balance of the game.

### What *is* allowed?
* **Highly Unbalanced Trades:** You **can** trade 4 sheep for 1 wheat if both players agree. While it feels like a giveaway, it is a legal trade because an exchange of different goods occurred.
* **Future Promises:** You can say, "If you give me this wheat now, I won't rob you later," or "I'll trade you back next turn." However, these are **non-binding** agreements. You are not legally required to fulfill a promise made for a future turn.

In Catan, that combination of pieces and achievements would be worth **7 Victory Points**.

Here is the breakdown of the scoring:

* **4 Settlements:** 4 VP ($1$ point each)
* **1 City:** 2 VP ($2$ points each)
* **Longest Road:** 2 VP
* **Total:** **7 VP**

Keep in mind that you need **10 Victory Points** to win a standard game of Catan. You are currently three points away—building two more cities or one city and buying the right Development Card (Victory Point) would get you there!

In **Catan**, it is actually impossible for two players to reach 10 Victory Points at the exact same time because points can only be claimed or validated during a player's **own turn**.

Here is how the victory condition works:

* **The Active Player Rule:** You can only win on your turn. Even if you reach 10 points during someone else’s turn (for example, by building the Longest Road because someone else’s road was broken), you do not win immediately. You must wait until your next turn begins to be declared the winner.
* **The "First to 10" Principle:** The first person to reach 10 points on their own turn wins instantly. Since players take turns sequentially, one person will always reach the threshold before the others.

### Exceptions and Edge Cases

| Scenario | Outcome |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Hidden Victory Points** | If you have Victory Point cards (Development Cards) that put you at 10 points, you must wait until your turn to reveal them and claim the win. |
| **Longest Road/Largest Army** | If an opponent loses the Longest Road and it passes to you during *their* turn, and that puts you at 10 points, the game continues. If the current player reaches 10 points later in that same turn, **they win**, even if you also have 10. |
| **Simultaneous 10 (Tournament Rules)** | In some very rare tournament scenarios where a move causes two people to hit 10 points simultaneously (and neither is the active player), the win goes to the person whose turn is **next** in clockwise order. |

In short, there are no tie-breakers because the turn-based nature of the game acts as a natural "first-past-the-post" system.

In **Catan**, Development cards **do not count** toward your hand size when the robber is moved.

According to the official rules, the discard penalty triggered by rolling a **7** only applies to **Resource cards**.

### The Rule Breakdown
* **Hand Limit:** If you have more than **7 Resource cards** when a 7 is rolled, you must choose half of them (rounded down) to discard.
* **Development Cards:** These are kept separate from your resource hand. They are never discarded due to a robber roll, and they cannot be stolen by an opponent moving the robber.

---

> **Note:** Only "Unplayed" Development cards are hidden from other players, but they still occupy a different category than the resources (brick, lumber, wool, grain, and ore) used for building.

Yes, in **Catan**, you can build a new settlement on an existing road, provided you follow the **Distance Rule** and the **Location Requirement**.

### 1. The Distance Rule
A settlement can only be placed on an intersection if all three adjacent intersections are currently **unoccupied**. This means there must be at least two "edges" (road segments) between any two settlements or cities, regardless of who owns them.

### 2. The Location Requirement
To build a settlement after the initial setup phase, it must be connected to at least one of your own existing roads. 

### Key Scenarios
* **Building on your own road:** This is the standard way to expand. You build a long road and place a settlement at an open intersection along that road to "break it up" and claim resources.
* **Building on an opponent's road:** You **cannot** build a settlement on a road segment owned by another player. However, if an opponent has built a long road but left an intersection empty (and it satisfies the Distance Rule), you could potentially build there if your own road also reaches that same intersection.

> **Note on "Breaking" Roads:** If you build a settlement on an intersection that lies in the middle of an opponent’s continuous road, you **break** their road for the purposes of the **Longest Road** card. The road segments remain on the board, but they no longer count as one continuous sequence.

In the board game **Root**, "turmoiling" for a fourth time—or any number of times—follows the same mechanical process. There isn't a special "four-strike" penalty or a hidden rule that changes the game state specifically for the fourth occurrence.

However, because the Eyrie Dynasties rely on momentum, a fourth turmoil usually signals a late-game crisis that can be devastating to your chances of winning.

---

### The Turmoil Process
When you cannot fulfill a card in your Decree, the following happens immediately:

1.  **Humiliation:** You lose **1 Victory Point** for every Bird card (including the Viziers) currently in your Decree.
2.  **Purge:** All cards in the Decree are discarded, except for your two starting Loyal Viziers.
3.  **Depose:** Your current Leader is removed from the game (flipped facedown). They cannot be used again for the rest of the match.
4.  **Reset:** Your turn ends immediately, and you skip straight to the Evening phase.

---

### Why the 4th Time is Unique
While the rules don't change, the **context** of a fourth turmoil is usually grim:

* **Leader Exhaustion:** The Eyrie has 4 Leaders (Charismatic, Builder, Despot, and Commander). If you turmoil for the fourth time, you have officially **exhausted your entire leadership pool**. 
* **The Great Reset:** According to the rules, once all four Leaders have been deposed, you flip them all back face-up and can choose from the full set again.
* **The Math of Losing:** By the fourth turmoil, you have likely lost a significant chunk of points from Bird cards. If you had 3 Bird cards in each Decree when you crashed, that’s -12 points over the course of the game.
* **Action Economy:** Since a turmoil ends your turn instantly, four turmoils mean you have essentially "lost" four entire turns of building or battling.

### Strategy Tip
If you find yourself hitting a fourth turmoil, it usually means your Decree became too "Bird-heavy" or you were unable to maintain a clear path to a clearing of a specific suit. At this stage of the game, other factions (like the Marquise or Alliance) have likely built an engine that is difficult to overcome with a fresh, empty Decree.

In the board game **Root**, the Corvid Conspiracy uses four distinct types of plot tokens. These are placed facedown on the map and later flipped to activate their unique effects.

Each plot has a specific impact on the board state:

### 1. Bombs
* **Effect:** When flipped, a Bomb removes **all** enemy pieces (warriors and buildings/tokens) in its clearing. 
* **Strategic Use:** It is the ultimate deterrent, forcing opponents to think twice about moving into or staying in a clearing controlled by the Corvids.

### 2. Snares
* **Effect:** A Snare prevents enemy pieces from **moving out** of the clearing and stops them from **placing** any tokens or buildings there.
* **Strategic Use:** This is excellent for trapping an opponent's large army or protecting a clearing from being built upon by factions like the Marquise de Cat or the Eyrie Dynasties.

### 3. Extortion
* **Effect:** When flipped, the Corvid player **draws a card** from the deck for each enemy player who has pieces in that clearing. 
* **Strategic Use:** This is the primary engine for hand management, allowing the Corvids to fuel further actions and crafting.

### 4. Raid
* **Effect:** When a Raid is flipped, it places **one Corvid warrior** in every adjacent clearing.
* **Strategic Use:** This is a mobility tool used to spread the Conspiracy across the board quickly without relying solely on the "Move" action.

---

### Plot Mechanics Summary
| Plot Type | Primary Function | Interaction |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Bomb** | Destruction | Clears the entire clearing of enemies. |
| **Snare** | Lockdown | Prevents movement and placement. |
| **Extortion** | Resources | Generates card draw from enemies. |
| **Raid** | Expansion | Spawns warriors in neighboring clearings. |

In **Root**, understanding the distinction between these three types of pieces is crucial because many cards and faction abilities (like "Battling" or "Moving") interact with them differently.

All three categories fall under the umbrella term **Pieces**. Here is the breakdown:

### 1. Units
**Units** are the mobile pieces on the board. Most importantly, **only units can Move and Battle.**
* **Warriors:** The standard soldiers for most factions (e.g., Marquise de Cat's orange cats, Eyrie Dynasties' blue birds).
* **The Vagabond:** The Vagabond pawn is considered a unit.
* **Key Rule:** Units generally provide **Rule** in a clearing. Most factions determine who rules a clearing by summing their total number of warriors and buildings.

### 2. Buildings
**Buildings** are square pieces that are placed in the specific **slots** (white boxes) found in clearings.
* **Examples:** Marquise Sawmills, Eyrie Roosts, or Lizard Cult Gardens.
* **Key Rule:** Buildings contribute to **Rule**. If a clearing has two of your warriors and one of your buildings, your total presence is 3. 
* **Vulnerability:** Buildings cannot move. If they are removed (usually through battle or special abilities), the attacker often scores **1 Victory Point**.

### 3. Tokens
**Tokens** are round pieces that are placed directly in a clearing but **do not** occupy the square building slots.
* **Examples:** Woodland Alliance Sympathy, Marquise Wood, or Corvid Conspiracy Plots.
* **Key Rule:** Tokens **do not** contribute to Rule (unless a specific faction ability states otherwise). 
* **Vulnerability:** Like buildings, if a token is removed, the person who removed it usually scores **1 Victory Point**.

---

### Comparison Summary

| Category | Shape | Occupies Slot? | Contributes to Rule? | Can Move? |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Units** | Standing Meeple | No | Yes | Yes |
| **Buildings** | Square | **Yes** | Yes | No |
| **Tokens** | Round | No | **No** | No |

### Important Distinction: "Items"
Note that the **Vagabond's Items** (swords, boots, tea) are not considered pieces on the board. They stay on the Vagabond's character board and cannot be attacked or removed via standard battle in the way units, buildings, or tokens are.

In **Root**, the short answer is **yes**, but with a very important distinction regarding *what* you remove.

Here is how the **Hostile** keyword triggers:

### 1. The Trigger
If you remove **any piece** (Warrior, Building, or Token) of another faction during **your turn**, that faction immediately becomes **Hostile**. 

* **Wait, any piece?** Yes. Even if you only remove a building or a token (like a Wood token or a Sympathy token), the relationship shifts to Hostile.
* **During your turn:** This is the key qualifier. If you remove a piece while defending (during another player’s turn), the relationship does **not** become Hostile.

### 2. The "Infamy" Reward
Once a faction is Hostile, your interactions with them change:
* **Victory Points:** You gain **+1 VP** for every piece of that Hostile faction you remove in battle (in addition to the standard +1 VP for buildings/tokens).
* **Movement:** Moving into a clearing containing any warriors of a Hostile faction costs **one extra Move action** (essentially an extra Boot).

### 3. Can you go back?
**No.** Once a faction becomes Hostile, it remains Hostile for the rest of the game. You can no longer move up their Relationship track to gain the standard rewards for gifting cards.

> **Important Rule Note:** If you remove a piece using an ability that isn't a direct "Strike" or "Battle" (such as the **favor cards** or certain character-specific actions), the relationship still becomes Hostile because you removed their piece during your turn.

In the board game **Root**, an Ambush card can be played at the very **beginning of a battle**, specifically during the **Step 1: Use Ambush Cards** phase.

Here is the breakdown of how and when they are used:

### Timing and Requirements
* **When:** Immediately after a player declares an attack, but **before** any dice are rolled.
* **Who:** The **Defender** typically plays an Ambush card first. However, if the Defender plays one, the **Attacker** may play a matching Ambush card to cancel it out.
* **Suit Match:** The suit of the Ambush card (Fox, Rabbit, Mouse) must match the clearing where the battle is taking place. A Bird Ambush card is wild and can be used in any clearing.

### How the Sequence Works
1.  **Defender's Option:** The Defender may play a matching Ambush card. If they do, and it isn't canceled, it immediately deals **2 hits** to the Attacker. 
2.  **Attacker’s Counter:** If the Defender plays an Ambush card, the Attacker can play a matching Ambush card from their own hand to **cancel** the Defender’s Ambush. No hits are dealt by either card in this case, and both cards are discarded.
3.  **Check for Defenselessness:** If the Defender’s Ambush removes all the Attacker’s warriors, the battle ends immediately before dice are even rolled.

---

> **Important Note:** You cannot play an Ambush card if you are **Defenseless** (meaning you have no warriors in the clearing). If you have no warriors, you simply cannot trigger the card's effect.

In **ROOT**, the interaction between the Alliance's **Revolt** and the **Lost City** landmark centers on how the landmark alters the rules of "presence" and movement.

### The Rule Interaction
The Lost City is treated as a **clearing of every suit** (Fox, Rabbit, and Mouse). Because a Revolt requires the Woodland Alliance to spend two supporters matching the clearing's suit, the interaction works as follows:

*   **Supporter Cost:** You can use **any two matching supporters** to revolt in the Lost City. For example, two Fox, two Rabbit, or two Mouse supporters will all work. However, you cannot "mix and match" (e.g., one Fox and one Mouse) because the Revolt action specifically requires two supporters of a suit matching the clearing.
*   **Removal of Pieces:** When the Revolt occurs, you remove all pieces of other players from the Lost City. Since the Lost City is often a high-traffic chokepoint, this can be devastating to opponents who are using it to bridge their movement between different quarters of the board.
*   **Base Placement:** Once the revolt is successful, you place the matching Alliance base. Because the Lost City is all suits, it effectively acts as a "wild" slot for whichever base you have available in your supply.

### Strategic Considerations
*   **Martial Law:** If another player has 3 or more warriors in the Lost City, Martial Law still applies. You would need to spend **three** matching supporters (instead of two) to revolt there.
*   **Adjacency:** The Lost City is often connected to more clearings than a standard clearing. Having an Alliance base there allows you to spread sympathy much more efficiently, as you have a wider range of "adjacent" clearings to target for your next Spread Sympathy actions.

---

Would you like a clarification on how the Lost City affects specific faction abilities, like the Eyrie's Decree or the Vagabond's movement?

In the base game and major expansions of **Root**, there is generally only one faction that can "lose" points already gained on the victory track:

### **1. The Eyrie Dynasties**
The Eyrie is the only faction that can lose victory points as a direct consequence of their mechanics. This happens when they fall into **Turmoil**. 
* **The Penalty:** If the Eyrie cannot fulfill their Decree, they immediately enter Turmoil. Part of this process involves losing **one victory point for every Bird card** (including the Loyal Viziers) currently in their Decree.
* **The Setback:** This is often a significant blow, especially if you have heavily loaded your Decree with Bird cards to make it more flexible.

### **2. The Vagabond (Coalitions)**
While not a "loss" in the traditional sense, the Vagabond can effectively **reset** their point total.
* **The Mechanic:** If a Vagabond plays a **Dominance Card** to form a coalition with the player in last place, they remove their score marker from the victory track and place it on that player's faction board. 
* **The Result:** From that point forward, they no longer score individual points and instead win or lose alongside their partner.

### **Common Misconceptions**
It’s a common question among newer players whether other factions lose points when their pieces are removed:
* **Marquise de Cat / Woodland Alliance:** They **do not** lose points when buildings or sympathy tokens are destroyed. However, the pieces return to their player boards, meaning they have to "re-earn" those points by building them again. 
* **The Thief/Vagabond:** Taking items from a Vagabond or damaging them does not reduce their victory points.
* **Card Effects:** Most card effects and battle results grant points to the *attacker*, but they do not subtract points from the *defender*.

---
> **Note:** If you are playing with Fan Factions or specific house rules, some custom setups might include point-loss mechanics, but in the official **Law of Root**, it is strictly an Eyrie problem!

In **ROOT**, the short answer is **no**. The Riverfolk Company cannot use the **Tunnels** dominance/persistent effect card to move between their Trade Posts.

Here is the mechanical breakdown of why this interaction doesn't work:

### 1. The Tunnels Card Requirement
The text of the **Tunnels** card specifically states:
> "You treat all clearings with your **buildings** as adjacent."

### 2. Trade Posts are Not Buildings
In the hierarchy of piece types in Root, there is a strict distinction between **Buildings** (square pieces) and **Tokens** (round pieces). 
* **Buildings:** Sawmills, Workshops, Recruitment Centers, Roosts, Bases, etc.
* **Tokens:** Wood, Keep, Sympathy, and **Trade Posts**.

Because Trade Posts are **Tokens**, they do not satisfy the condition of the Tunnels card. Even if you have Trade Posts in two different clearings, they are not "buildings," so the Tunnels effect does not bridge them.

### 3. Riverfolk Movement Rules
The Riverfolk Company is already one of the most mobile factions because they can move via the **River** as if it were a path (and they always treat river-connected clearings as adjacent). 

To move between non-adjacent clearings that aren't on the river, the Riverfolk must follow standard movement rules:
* You must **Rule** either the origin clearing or the destination clearing.
* The clearings must be connected by a printed **Path**.

### Summary Table: Buildings vs. Tokens
| Faction | Buildings (Works with Tunnels) | Tokens (Does NOT work) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Riverfolk** | None | Trade Posts |
| **Marquise** | Sawmill, Workshop, Recruiter | Keep, Wood |
| **Eyrie** | Roosts | None |
| **Alliance** | Bases | Sympathy |
| **Underground Duchy** | Citadels, Markets | Tunnels |

Does this help clarify the movement options for your current game, or are you looking for other ways to increase the Riverfolk's reach?

In **Root**, specifically when playing with the **Riverfolk Company**, the rules for using Mercenaries and Dominance cards have very specific timing constraints.

### The Short Answer: No
You cannot purchase mercenaries and use them to check for a Dominance victory on the **same turn**.

---

### The Rules Breakdown

**1. The Dominance Victory Check**
To win with a Dominance card, you must fulfill the condition (ruling the specified clearings) at the **start of your Birdsong**.

**2. The Purchase Phase**
Purchasing services from the Riverfolk Company (like Mercenaries) occurs during your **Birdsong**. However, the sequence is critical:
* **Step 1:** Check for Victory (Dominance).
* **Step 2:** All other Birdsong actions (including purchasing services).

Because you must already rule the clearings when your turn begins to trigger the win, any mercenaries bought *during* that Birdsong come into play too late to help you meet the check for that specific turn.

**3. The Persistence of Services**
Mercenaries last until the **end of your turn**. 
* If you buy them on **Turn A**, they help you rule clearings for your Daylight and Evening actions of Turn A.
* They return to the Riverfolk at the end of Turn A.
* By the start of **Turn B** (your next Birdsong), you no longer have them.

### Summary Table
| Action | Timing |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Check Dominance Win** | Start of Birdsong |
| **Purchase Mercenaries** | During Birdsong |
| **Mercenaries Expire** | End of current turn |

> **Note:** While Mercenaries are excellent for helping you *battle* into a clearing or establishing rule to build during your Daylight phase, they are structurally designed to be temporary "muscle" that cannot be held overnight to satisfy the start-of-turn Dominance requirement.

Yes, you can use the **Scorched Earth** action on the clearing containing the **Marquise's Keep**.

However, there is a specific interaction you should keep in mind regarding how the Keep is removed:

### 1. The Action
When you perform Scorched Earth, you remove all enemy pieces in your clearing. Since the Keep is a token, it is removed from the board along with any buildings or warriors present.

### 2. The "No Placement" Rule
The most significant impact of doing this is the permanent effect on the clearing. After Scorched Earth is used:
* The clearing is marked with a **scorched earth marker**.
* **No pieces** (buildings, tokens, or warriors) can be placed or moved into that clearing by any player for the rest of the game.

### 3. The Keep's Special Property
Usually, the Keep is unique because the Marquise is the only player who can place pieces in its clearing. By scorching it, you aren't just removing her "home base" advantage; you are effectively deleting that clearing from the map for everyone. Because the Keep cannot be replaced once removed, the Marquise loses her respawn point (the Field Hospital ability) for the remainder of the match.

---

> **Note:** Since Scorched Earth "removes" the pieces rather than "battling" them, the Marquise cannot use **Field Hospitals** to save any warriors caught in the blast.

No, in **Splendor**, a player can only acquire **one noble tile per turn**.

If you fulfill the requirements for more than one noble at the end of your turn, you must choose which one to take. The other(s) remain on the board, and you (or another player) can potentially claim them on a future turn, provided the requirements are still met.

### Key Rules for Nobles
* **Automatic Action:** Acquiring a noble is not considered an "action." It happens automatically at the end of your turn after you have completed your main action (taking tokens or buying/reserving a card).
* **Requirement:** To qualify, you must have the required number of **bonuses** (the diamond icons on cards you have already purchased). Tokens in your hand do not count toward noble requirements.
* **Permanence:** Once you take a noble tile, it stays in front of you until the end of the game. They are worth **3 prestige points** each.

In **Splendor**, you "win" a noble by accumulating specific **bonuses** through the development cards you have already purchased. Unlike cards, you do not "buy" nobles with tokens; they visit you automatically once you meet their criteria.

Here is the breakdown of how the process works:

### 1. Check the Requirements
Each noble tile displays a specific combination of card bonuses required to attract them. 
* **Example:** A noble might require **4 Blue bonuses** and **4 White bonuses**.
* Only the **diamond icons** at the top right of the cards you’ve already played in front of you count toward this total. 
* **Note:** Gem tokens in your hand do **not** count toward noble requirements.

### 2. The Timing
The visit happens at the **very end of your turn**. 
* You first perform your main action (taking tokens, reserving a card, or buying a card).
* Once your action is finished, you check if your total card bonuses match or exceed the requirements on any available noble tile.

### 3. Claiming the Tile
* If you meet the requirements, you take the noble tile and place it in front of you.
* Each noble is worth **3 prestige points**.
* **The "One per Turn" Rule:** As mentioned before, if you happen to qualify for two different nobles simultaneously, you may only take **one**. You'll have to wait until the end of your next turn to claim the second one.

### 4. Competitive Strategy
Because nobles are limited (the number of nobles available is always the **number of players + 1**), they often act as a race. If another player reaches the requirements for a specific noble before you do, they take it, and it is no longer available for the rest of the game.

No, you cannot.

According to the official rules of **Splendor**, you may only take two gem tokens of the same color if there are at least **4 tokens available** in that color stack before you take them.

### Quick Reference: Taking Tokens
* **3 Different Colors:** You can take 3 tokens of different colors regardless of how many are in the stacks (as long as at least one is available in each).
* **2 Same Colors:** Only allowed if there are **4 or more** tokens of that color remaining.
* **Gold (Wilds):** You can only acquire a gold token by taking the "Reserve a Card" action.

If there are only 3 tokens of a specific color left, your only option for that color is to include one of them as part of a "3 different colors" set.

In a standard game of **Splendor**, there are **40 tokens** in total.

The distribution of these tokens changes depending on how many people are playing, but the total box count remains the same. Here is the breakdown:

### **Total Box Contents**
* **7 Emerald** (Green)
* **7 Sapphire** (Blue)
* **7 Ruby** (Red)
* **7 Diamond** (White)
* **7 Onyx** (Black)
* **5 Gold** (Yellow/Jokers)

---

### **Setup by Player Count**
While the gold tokens always stay at **5**, you remove some of the colored gem tokens for smaller groups:

| Player Count | Tokens per Gem Color | Total Tokens Used |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **4 Players** | 7 of each color | 40 tokens |
| **3 Players** | 5 of each color | 30 tokens |
| **2 Players** | 4 of each color | 25 tokens |

> **Note:** Regardless of the player count, the **10-token limit** per player remains the same. If you finish your turn with more than 10, you must return the excess to the bank.

In **Splendor**, you can purchase any face-up development card currently on the table or any development card you have previously **reserved** in your hand.

On your turn, you may buy a card if you can pay the required cost shown in the lower-left corner using your collected gem tokens and any bonuses from cards you already own. Here is the breakdown of the options:

### 1. Face-Up Development Cards
There are three levels of cards available in the common play area:
* **Level 1 (Green):** The bottom row, usually costing 3–5 tokens.
* **Level 2 (Yellow):** The middle row, usually costing 5–8 tokens.
* **Level 3 (Blue):** The top row, which are the most expensive but offer the most prestige points.

### 2. Reserved Cards
If you used a previous turn to **Reserve** a card (taking it from the board or drawing it blindly from a deck into your hand), you can spend your turn to play that card by paying its cost. 

---

### Key Buying Rules
* **One Action per Turn:** Buying a card counts as your entire action for the turn. You cannot buy a card and also take tokens or reserve another card.
* **The Bonus System:** Each card you have already purchased provides a permanent "bonus" of a specific gem type. This acts as a discount for all future purchases. 
* **Gold Tokens (Jokers):** If you have gold tokens (wilds), you can use them to replace any gem color you are missing to complete a purchase.
* **Replenishing the Board:** If you buy a face-up card, a new card from the corresponding deck is immediately flipped over to replace it.

No, you are **not** allowed to take just two tokens of different colors in that specific scenario.

According to the official rules of **Splendor**, when you choose the action to take tokens of different colors, you must take exactly **three** tokens of different colors. 

### The Rule Breakdown
* **The Three-Token Rule:** You must take three tokens of three different colors. 
* **The Exception:** You can only take fewer than three tokens if there are **fewer than three colors remaining** in the entire bank. 
* **The Restriction:** Even if you can only take two tokens (because only two colors remain), you still cannot take two tokens of the same color unless that color has **at least 4 tokens** available in its stack.

### What should you do?
If you cannot fulfill the requirement of taking three different colors, and you cannot take two of the same color (due to the "4 in stock" rule), you must choose a different action for your turn:
1.  **Reserve a card:** Take a gold (joker) token and put a development card into your hand.
2.  **Purchase a card:** If you have the resources, buy a card from the board or your hand.

Essentially, if the board state prevents you from taking the required number of gems, that specific "Take Gems" action is unavailable to you.

Yes, you can spend **4 gold tokens** to buy a card that costs **4 gems**.

Since gold tokens function as "wild" jokers, they can replace any color. As long as you have the tokens in your supply, you can use them to cover any portion of a card's cost—or even the entire cost—provided you don't exceed the 10-token inventory limit while you are holding them.

### A Quick Reminder on the "Reserve" Action
Because gold tokens are so flexible, remember that you can only acquire them by **reserving a card**. If you want to use 4 gold tokens for a single purchase, you would have had to perform the "Reserve" action four separate times earlier in the game to collect them.

In **Splendor**, the rules regarding reserved cards are specific about what is public and what is private:

* **The Card Identity:** The card itself is kept **private**. You should keep it face-down or in your hand so that opponents do not know which specific development card you have reserved.
* **The Count:** The **number of cards** you have reserved is **public** information. Since you are limited to a maximum of three reserved cards at any time, other players must be able to see how many you are currently holding.

### Summary of Information Visibility

| Element | Visibility |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Development Cards in Hand** | Private (Face-down) |
| **Number of Reserved Cards** | Public (Countable) |
| **Total Prestige Points** | Public |
| **Gem Tokens & Bonuses** | Public |

While your opponents won't know exactly what you're planning to build, they will always know how many "slots" you have left for future reservations.

In **Splendor**, it is technically impossible for a player to be unable to perform at least one of the three main actions. Because of how the game is structured, you will always have a legal move available.

Here is why you can always act:

* **Taking Tokens:** Even if you have the maximum of **10 tokens** in your hand, you can still take tokens from the bank and then immediately discard down to 10. If the bank is completely empty (which is rare), you still have the other two actions.
* **Reserving a Card:** You can always reserve a face-up card from the middle or the top card of any of the three decks, provided you have fewer than **3 cards** already reserved. Even if there is no gold left in the bank, you can still take the card into your hand.
* **Purchasing a Card:** If you have the resources, you can always buy a card from the board or your reserved pile.

### The "Stall" Rule
If for some reason you cannot take tokens (the bank is empty) and you cannot reserve a card (you already have 3 reserved), you would be forced to **purchase a card**. If you also cannot afford to purchase any card on the board or in your hand, the game state would be effectively frozen—however, the math of the game ensures that the bank and the board always provide an "out" (usually reserving) before this point is ever reached.

In the extremely rare competitive edge-case where the bank is empty of all tokens and you already have three cards reserved, you **must** purchase a card. If you cannot do that, you have reached a state not explicitly covered by the rulebook, but standard tournament play dictates you would simply pass, though this is virtually impossible in a standard game.

In **Splendor**, you actually don’t have much of a choice; the game’s economy is structured so that your permanent bonuses (cards) are always applied first, and tokens are used to fill the remaining gap.

Here is the breakdown of how the payment hierarchy works:

## The Payment Rule
When you purchase a development card from the center or from your hand (if reserved), you must satisfy the cost shown in the bottom-left corner of that card.

* **Step 1: Apply Your Bonuses.** Count the number of development cards you have already purchased that match the color of the cost. Each card acts as a **permanent -1 discount** for that color.
* **Step 2: Pay with Tokens.** If your bonuses do not cover the full cost, you must pay the remaining amount using your gem tokens.
* **Step 3: Use Gold (Jokers).** If you are still short, you may use gold tokens to stand in for any color.

---

### Key Clarifications

* **You cannot "save" bonuses:** If you have 3 Blue bonuses and a card costs 2 Blue, your bonuses cover it completely. You cannot choose to pay with 2 Blue tokens instead just to keep your cards "unused" for later—bonuses are passive and always active.
* **Tokens are returned:** Any tokens you use to pay for a card are returned to the common piles in the center of the table.
* **Card permanence:** Once you buy a development card, it stays in your play area for the rest of the game. It is never "spent" or discarded to pay for another card.

> **Summary:** You always use your cards (bonuses) first. You only use gem tokens to cover whatever the cards don't already pay for.
